prompt: What search mode should I use for different types of queries? Provide guidance mapping scenarios to search modes.
response:
Good question — the “best” search mode depends on what you’re trying to find and how precise the result must be. Below is a practical mapping: scenarios → recommended search mode(s) → why, plus quick examples/operators you can use.

General quick reference
- Quick factual answer (definition, conversion, short fact)
  - Mode: General web / semantic search (regular search engine or AI answer)
  - Why: You want a concise, high-confidence answer; engines synthesize top sources.
  - Tips: Plain query, or “What is X” / “How many” / “Convert 10 km to miles”.

- Very recent/breaking news
  - Mode: News search (Google News, Bing News, dedicated news tab)
  - Why: Filters for recency and news sources; avoids stale pages.
  - Tips: Use time filters (past hour/day/week); include location or entity names.

- In-depth research / comprehensive coverage
  - Mode: Scholarly/academic search (Google Scholar, Semantic Scholar, PubMed) + regular web for background
  - Why: Gives peer-reviewed papers, citations, and authoritative literature.
  - Tips: Use author/title/date filters, site:.edu or filetype:pdf for papers.

- Statistical data / official numbers
  - Mode: Government / statistical portals (site:.gov, OECD, World Bank) or data portals
  - Why: Official sources are preferred for accuracy and provenance.
  - Tips: Use site:countrycode.gov or filetype:xls/csv; include year.

- Product shopping / price comparison
  - Mode: Shopping search / dedicated marketplaces (Google Shopping, Amazon, price-comparison sites)
  - Why: Shows prices, sellers, reviews, and availability.
  - Tips: Use filters (price, ratings), include model numbers, use “compare” or “best [product]” queries.

- Local businesses / directions / hours
  - Mode: Maps/local search (Google Maps, Apple Maps, Yelp)
  - Why: Returns maps, hours, reviews, and route planning.
  - Tips: Search by neighborhood + business type; use “open now” filters.

- Images / visual matching
  - Mode: Image search and reverse image search (Google Images, Bing Images, TinEye, Google Lens)
  - Why: Find pictures, identify objects, or find image origins/duplicates.
  - Tips: Upload image for reverse search; use color/size filters.

- Videos / tutorials
  - Mode: Video search (YouTube, Vimeo, platform-specific)
  - Why: Best for demonstrations, lectures, and visual tutorials.
  - Tips: Use channel filters, time length, or “how to” in query.

- Code snippets, libraries, programming help
  - Mode: Code search / developer resources (GitHub search, Stack Overflow, Sourcegraph)
  - Why: Source code, examples, and Q&A are located in these places.
  - Tips: Include language name, error message, and site:stackoverflow.com or repo:owner/name.

- Troubleshooting / error messages
  - Mode: Exact-match / site-specific search (Stack Overflow, vendor docs)
  - Why: Error texts are often discussed on Q&A sites or docs.
  - Tips: Quote the exact error string in quotes; include OS/version.

- Finding pages on a specific site
  - Mode: site: operator (site:example.com query)
  - Why: Limits search to one domain.
  - Tips: Combine with intitle:, inurl:, or filetype:.

- Exact phrase or headline lookup
  - Mode: Exact-match search (quotes)
  - Why: Forces phrase order and exact wording.
  - Tips: Use "exact phrase" and minus words to exclude.

- Broader research / concept discovery
  - Mode: Semantic / exploration mode (natural language queries, AI/semantic search)
  - Why: Finds conceptually related results, not just keyword matches.
  - Tips: Ask open-ended questions, use “related topics” or “alternatives to X.”

- Legal documents and patents
  - Mode: Legal/patent databases (Google Patents, USPTO, LexisNexis)
  - Why: Specialized search and official filings.
  - Tips: Use patent numbers, classifications, and assignee names.

- Social media content / posts
  - Mode: Platform-specific search or third-party social search (Twitter/X search, Facebook, LinkedIn)
  - Why: Social platforms index posts differently; use native search.
  - Tips: Use “from:username” or site:twitter.com for cross-site search.

Search-operator cheat sheet (use with regular search engines)
- Exact phrase: "…" (quotes)
- Exclude term: -word
- Either/or: OR (capitalized)
- Site limit: site:domain.com
- File type: filetype:pdf, filetype:xls
- Title-only: intitle:word
- URL-only: inurl:word
- Wildcard: * (fill-in)
- Time filter: tools → past hour/day/year (or time: in some engines)

Quick decision flow
- Want a quick factual answer → regular web/semantic search.
- Need authoritative research → scholar/official sources.
- Want recent developments → news mode/time filters.
- Need images or identify something visually → image/reverse image search.
- Looking locally → maps/local search.
- Troubleshooting or code → site-specific developer resources and exact-match queries.
- Want conceptual matches/ideas → semantic/AI-assisted search.

If you tell me a few concrete scenarios you run into frequently, I can map those specifically to search modes and give exact example queries/operators.
